title: The Last Sunday after the Epiphany
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## Sunday, February 23, 2020 | Preacher: Richard Lawson

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

This is the Last Sunday after the Epiphany (also called Transfiguration Sunday in some traditions), falling seven weeks before Easter in 2020. The Revised Common Lectionary readings for this date would be 2 Kings 2:1-12 (Elijah's ascension), Psalm 50:1-6, 2 Corinthians 4:3-6, and Mark 9:2-9 (the Transfiguration), with the latter two forming the core of the gospel message. This Sunday concludes the Epiphany season and marks the transition toward Lent, which would begin nine days later on Ash Wednesday, March 4, 2020.

EPISCOPAL/ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was in a relatively stable period following the 2018 General Convention, though discussions around LGBTQ+ inclusion and marriage rites continued in various dioceses. The Diocese of Colorado had no major episcopal vacancies or elections at this time, remaining under the leadership of Bishop Robert O'Neill who had served since 2008. Saint John's Cathedral itself, as the diocesan seat, would have been engaged with standard cathedral programming and diocesan coordination without exceptional controversies or announcements in late February 2020.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver had experienced typical late winter weather in the week prior, with mild temperatures in the 40s-50s Fahrenheit following some snow earlier in the month. The Colorado legislative session was in full swing at the State Capitol (located near downtown Denver and the cathedral's Capitol Hill neighborhood), with various bills under consideration including education funding and energy policy. No major breaking news stories dominated local coverage in the immediate week before February 23, though homelessness and housing affordability remained ongoing civic concerns in downtown Denver.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The COVID-19 pandemic was beginning to receive significant American media attention following confirmed cases in Washington State and California, though it was not yet the dominant national story—most Americans remained focused on the 2020 presidential primary race, which was accelerating with Nevada caucuses held on February 22 (the day before this sermon) showing strong support for Bernie Sanders. Michael Bloomberg and Joe Biden were competing vigorously for moderate support as Super Tuesday (March 3) approached. The stock market remained strong, with the S&P 500 near all-time highs despite emerging concerns about pandemic economic impacts.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The COVID-19 outbreak in China and spreading cases in South Korea, Italy, and Iran were receiving increased global media coverage as health officials warned of potential pandemic spread. The situation in Syria remained active with Russian-backed Syrian forces advancing against Turkish-supported rebels, creating a significant humanitarian crisis. The U.S.-Taliban peace negotiations in Afghanistan had recently produced a framework agreement (signed February 29, just days after this sermon), though details were still emerging.

CULTURAL MOMENT

American society existed in a state of pre-pandemic normalcy, with most people unaware of the magnitude of disruption that would arrive within weeks. The 2020 presidential primary dominated casual conversation and media consumption, creating a sense of political momentum and engagement. The Oscars ceremony had just concluded on February 9 with "Parasite" making history as the first non-English language film to win Best Picture, representing a culturally significant moment being discussed in arts circles. Denver's cultural institutions and the broader national mood retained a sense of continuity and routine, with no premonition of the social disruption that Lent 2020 would bring.
